column2:
For air travel, distances between airports are calculated using the great circle distance method. For road and rail, actual travel networks are used when available; otherwise, the great circle distance is applied as a fallback.
The API considers the direct and indirect emissions related to the travel.

These are examples of the different Activity-Based Travel inputs you could use, all demonstrating how the API deals with different types of inputs and how even small changes can effect the estimated emissions. 
You can use these sample inputs in the Excel Add-In, Postman Collection and in your own Python Scripts.
We also provide an analysis of these inputs to provide a full picture on how the API handles these inputs.

column11: This column will unpack the high-level expected results you will see when you input this sample data into the API

A short-haul flight is taken from Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport to Berlin Brandenburg Airport, with the flight class specified as economy. Economy class allows for greater passenger density, so emissions per person are lower. However, short-haul flights are relatively less efficient on a per-kilometer basis due to the high fuel burn during takeoff and landing phases, which make up a larger share of the total journey. The 2022 reference year ensures that recent emission factors are used.

This is a long-haul intercontinental flight from Cape Town to Berlin, and the flight class is changed to business. This results in a substantial increase in emissions. Business class passengers account for significantly more space and weight per seat, leading to 2–3x higher emissions per person. Additionally, the longer distance increases total fuel consumption. While the year 2021 is slightly earlier, it has minimal influence compared to the impact of flight distance and class. The emissions in this case will be significantly higher than in the Paris–Berlin economy example above.

This journey involves a large petrol-powered vehicle, which is among the least efficient car configurations. The larger car size increases fuel consumption per kilometer, and petrol produces high Scope 1 emissions per litre burned. Since the trip occurred in 2018, older emission factors and less efficient average vehicle performance may apply. This scenario sets a high-emission benchmark for comparison due to both vehicle size and fossil fuel dependency.

In contrast to the above example, this journey uses a small battery electric vehicle. The smaller vehicle reduces energy consumption, and being fully electric, it produces zero direct emissions (Scope 1). Emissions instead depend on the carbon intensity of the Austrian electricity grid, which is known to be relatively low due to a high share of renewables. Additionally, the 2020 reference year means newer, more efficient vehicle technology may be reflected. Overall, this trip is expected to result in substantially lower emissions, driven by improvements in vehicle type, energy source, and efficiency.

This rail journey remains within Germany and Austria, both of which have highly electrified railway networks and relatively low-carbon electricity. Rail is inherently more efficient than road or air, and the relatively short-to-medium distance of this journey means emissions are minimal. Because the route is well-covered by known rail infrastructure, the calculation uses rail-based routing, avoiding fallback approximations. This represents a very low-emission transport case.

Despite covering a longer distance and crossing multiple national borders, this journey benefits from Sweden’s exceptionally clean electricity grid and high-quality European rail infrastructure. The emissions are surprisingly low, likely due to efficient electrified segments through central Europe and minimal fallback usage. This reflects the impact of clean electricity and well-covered rail networks, even over long-haul routes.
